topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail slot clattered as I yanked the heavy metal door open, expecting junk flyers and the usual deluge of circulars. Instead, something thin and stiff slid out onto the welcome mat. It was a card, folded precisely, bearing no name, no address, just a cheerful, almost aggressively bright script wishing 'Happy Birthday' to an unknown recipient. My jaw tightened almost instantly. I picked it up, the paper feeling offensively smooth under my thumb. Who in the world had the audacity to send this? I turned it over, searching for any identifying mark, any clue to the sender’s identity, and found nothing but blank white space. A slow, hot flush crept up my neck. I dropped it back into the box as if it were contaminated, my hands trembling slightly as I slammed the slot shut. This was an invasion, a deliberate, baffling gesture into my space, and I felt a deep, churning resentment bubble up in my chest.

[story 2]

I was midway through a particularly tedious afternoon, trying to ignore the incessant buzzing of my phone, when I noticed the anomaly. Tucked between a utility bill and a catalog, there it was: a birthday card, completely unaddressed. The handwriting was elegant, looping, and utterly anonymous. My breath hitched. I snatched it up, my fingers gripping the card so tightly the edges crinkled. My eyes scanned the front, searching for a signature, a return address, anything to anchor this strange object to reality. There was nothing. Just the sentiment, stark and unearned. A sharp, unpleasant sensation tightened my throat. I stared at the card, my knuckles turning white where I held it. It felt like a deliberate slight, a poorly executed prank aimed directly at my personal routine. I crumpled the paper once, twice, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet room.

[story 3]

The morning light was pale, and I was retrieving the mail when I saw it—a brightly colored card resting squarely in the center of the stack. Unsigned. Just a generic birthday wish. A wave of intense irritation washed over me, making my shoulders tense up beneath my sweater. I picked it up, holding it up to the light, scrutinizing every crease and fold. It was baffling. Who thought this was appropriate? The sheer lack of consideration was staggering. I felt a sudden, hot prickle behind my eyes. I didn't want it. I didn't want the implication that someone had taken the time to send something so presumptuous without even bothering to sign their name. With a decisive, jerky motion, I folded the card into a tight, angry square and shoved it deep into the bottom of the recycling bin, making sure it was completely hidden from view.
